params.text:
After an incident: report and record

When you work alone, the first response to an incident is yours. It might be aggression from a person you support or a relative, an accident in someone's home, a road or dog incident on the way to a call, or a threat outside a property. A near miss is different only in its ending — something that could easily have caused harm but happened not to this time. Both must be reported. Near misses are the cheapest warning your organisation will ever get, and they are usually the last one before someone is hurt.

  • Make yourself safe first: leave the property, get to your car or a public space, and put distance between you and the risk before you do anything else. You do not need permission to leave a situation you believe is unsafe.
  • Get medical attention if you need it: do not talk yourself out of it because you feel fine or the round is running late. Some injuries, including bites, scratches and head knocks, look minor at the time.
  • Ring your manager or the on-call line straight away: not at the end of the round and not on your next shift. They may need to arrange cover, warn the next worker due to attend, or contact the family.
  • Write it down while it is fresh: record the date, time and place, exactly what happened and in what order, who was present, what was said and done, any injury or damage, and who you told. Stick to facts and direct quotes rather than opinions about why someone behaved as they did.
  • Use the organisation's accident and incident form: complete it the same day if you can, sign and date it, and keep to the process your employer sets out rather than a text message that leaves no record.

params.alt: A care worker phoning their manager after an incident

params.text:
Reporting beyond your organisation

Some incidents also have to go outside the service. You are not expected to judge these alone — your job is to report accurately and promptly so your manager can act.

  • RIDDOR 2013: certain work-related events must be reported to the HSE by the responsible person, normally your employer — not by you as an individual worker. These include deaths, specified injuries such as fractures other than to fingers, thumbs or toes, an injury that stops you doing your normal work for more than seven consecutive days, listed dangerous occurrences, and certain occupational diseases. An act of physical violence to a worker is reportable where it causes one of those outcomes; abuse or threats alone are not RIDDOR matters, though they still go through your internal process.
  • The police: where a crime has occurred — assault, sexual assault, theft, criminal damage, threats to kill — it should be reported. You can report it yourself, and your employer should support you to do so. Preserve anything relevant and avoid cleaning up or moving items where you can.
  • Safeguarding: if the incident raises concerns about the safety of the person you support, another adult at risk, or a child in the household, a safeguarding referral goes to the local authority under your safeguarding policy. Do not wait to see whether it happens again.
  • Duty of candour: where a person using the service has been harmed, CQC Regulation 20 requires the provider to tell them what happened, apologise and explain. Regulation 12 requires safe care and treatment, which is why incidents feed straight back into risk assessment.

params.question: A relative pushes you during a visit and you injure your wrist. Your GP signs you off for ten days. What should happen about RIDDOR?

answers.text: Your employer, as the responsible person, reports it to the HSE because the injury kept you off your normal work for more than seven days.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Physical violence to a worker is reportable when it causes death, a specified injury, or more than seven consecutive days of incapacity. The report is made by the responsible person — normally your employer — not by you.

answers.text: You must submit the RIDDOR report to the HSE yourself within ten days.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. Individual workers do not make RIDDOR reports. Your duty is to report the incident promptly and accurately to your employer, who then decides and makes any HSE report.

answers.text: Nothing is reportable, because RIDDOR does not cover violence from another person.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. An act of non-consensual physical violence to someone at work is treated as a work-related accident under RIDDOR when it results in a reportable injury.

answers.text: It only becomes reportable if the police decide to charge the relative.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. RIDDOR duties are separate from any criminal investigation. The reporting decision turns on the injury and its work-relatedness, not on the outcome of a police case.
